children.text: Welcome! I’m an AI assistant created by the Executive AI Institute to develop a tailored AI Leadership Roadmap for your organization.
Over the next 3–5 minutes, you’ll answer a series of diagnostic multiple-choice questions about your leadership mindset, team readiness, past experiences, and AI ambitions.
Let's get to know each other a bit. What's your name?
Are you more likely to upskill your AI leadership skills yourself or coordinate an organizational initiative?
What is your organization's name? (Please do not include special characters like / or @)
In a few sentences, describe your organization’s mission, industry and scale.
Do you have a website for your organization that you can share? This will help this AI tailor the AI Leadership Roadmap to your sector.
Please enter your organizations website?
Finally, to which email address would you like this AI assistant to send your AI Leadership Roadmap?
Our AI Leadership Roadmap assesses 10 key areas.
- AI Leadership Maturity
- Organizational AI Maturity
- AI Change Adoption
- AI Leadership Effectiveness
- Leadership AI Understanding
- AI Leadership Blindspots
- AI Training & Capability
- AI Opportunities
- AI Inaction Risk Forecast
- Recommendations

{{name}}, this isn’t a test—there are no right or wrong answers. Instead, your responses will reveal your organization’s strengths, gaps and unique challenges. At the end, I’ll use that insight to deliver data-driven analysis and targeted recommendations, so you can confidently plan your next steps on the AI journey.
How confident are you in explaining basic AI concepts (e.g., machine learning, neural networks) to your team?
How crucial is AI to your organization’s future success?
Which step has your organization already taken toward AI?
How much time can your team spare for testing new digital tools?
Which of these describes your biggest daily friction point?
How easy is it for your team to pick up new digital skills?
How do you feel your company reacts when you mention “we should try AI”?

items.content: Open with caution – need more info

children.text: How often do teams share tips and help each other with new tools?
How would you rate your own understanding of AI?
How do you feel when you think of AI helping at work?
To what extent do you agree with this statement:
“AI will transform the workforce over the next three years more profoundly than the internet did in its first twenty.”
How often do you invite outside experts or skeptics to question your AI plans?
How do your leaders feel about adopting AI?
Which best describes your current AI knowledge?

items.content: Deep understanding of strategy and ethics

children.text: Looking back, how often have your organization change efforts failed?
If an initial AI pilot misses its targets, how likely are you to revise strategy and try again?
When facing a new technology choice, your leadership typically decides by:
How do you allocate resources for change management and training?
How do you gather user feedback on new tools or processes?
Which AI leadership area do you feel least prepared to address?
How well can your team articulate why AI matters to your top business goals?

items.content: Broadly discussed but not fully aligned

children.text: In past digital projects, how quickly did your team become comfortable using a new tool?
Which success metric matters most for an AI project?
How do you treat mistakes made by a new tool?
How well do you understand your organization’s current data, technology, and process limits for deploying AI?
How frequently do you run hands-on AI upskilling sessions (workshops, labs or sprints) for non-technical staff?
Which best describes your approach to managing AI-related risks?

items.content: Written policies exist but aren’t consistently enforced

children.text: When planning AI initiatives, how often do you link them to specific KPIs or ROI targets?

items.content: Always, as part of the standard process

children.text: How does your organization treat mistakes during a new project?
What part of your daily work feels slow or stuck and might improve with a smart tool?
How willing are you to partner with external experts on AI?
How do you feel your company reacts when you mention “we should try AI”?

items.content: Open with caution – need more info

children.text: How often do teams share tips and help each other with new tools?
What best describes your leadership team’s current AI maturity?

items.content: Clear AI strategy with limited alignment
Formalized policies and oversight in place

children.text: What best describes your organization’s overall AI journey?
At what stage of change adoption is your organization regarding AI?

items.content: Most people don’t see AI’s relevance
We acknowledge AI but aren’t comfortable yet
Pilots are running but not scaling
AI is embedded in our culture

children.text: I'm preparing the detailed report that will be sent to your email shortly. In the meantime, here is a overview of your AI Leadership Roadmap.

webhook.body:
Organization Name: Example

Organization Website: bykov-brett.net

How confident are you in explaining basic AI concepts (e.g., machine learning, neural networks) to your team? – C) Fairly confident

How crucial is AI to your organization’s future success? – B) Important

Which step has your organization already taken toward AI? – B) Informal research or discussion

How much time can your team spare for testing new digital tools? – C) A fair amount

Which of these describes your biggest daily friction point? – A) Gathering or cleaning data

How easy is it for your team to pick up new digital skills? – D) Very easy

How do you feel your company reacts when you mention “we should try AI”? – B) Open with caution – need more info

How often do teams share tips and help each other with new tools? – C) Often

How would you rate your own understanding of AI? – B) 2 – Some awareness

How do you feel when you think of machines helping at work? – A) Very excited

To what extent do you agree with this statement: “AI will transform the workforce over the next three years more profoundly than the internet did in its first twenty.” – C) Somewhat agree

How often do you invite outside experts or skeptics to question your AI plans? – B) Rarely

How do your leaders feel about adopting AI? – A) Very enthusiastic

Which best describes your current AI knowledge? – C) Moderate understanding of uses/limits

Looking back, how often have your change efforts failed? – B) Occasionally

If an initial AI pilot misses its targets, how likely are you to revise strategy and try again? – C) Somewhat likely

When facing a new technology choice, your leadership typically decides by what style? – B) Collaborative discussion

How do you allocate resources for change management and training? – C) Some dedicated resources

How do you gather user feedback on new tools or processes? – D) Structured feedback process with follow-up

Which AI leadership area do you feel least prepared to address? – B) Establishing governance and ethics

How well can your team articulate why AI matters to your top business goals? – C) Broadly discussed but not fully aligned

In past digital projects, how quickly did your team become comfortable using a new tool? – B) Fast (1–2 weeks)

Which success metric matters most for an AI project? – A) Revenue growth

How do you treat mistakes made by a new tool? – C) Hold a quick lessons-learned

How well do you understand your organization’s current data, technology, and process limits for deploying AI? – B) A little

How frequently do you run hands-on AI upskilling sessions (workshops, labs or sprints) for non-technical staff? – C) Quarterly

Which best describes your approach to managing AI-related risks? – D) Automated monitoring and regular audits

When planning AI initiatives, how often do you link them to specific KPIs or ROI targets? – B) Sometimes, on an ad-hoc basis

How does your organization treat mistakes during a new project? – D) Celebrates learning

What part of your daily work feels slow or stuck and might improve with a smart tool? – A) Gathering or cleaning data

How willing are you to partner with external experts on AI? – C) Open to partnerships

How do you feel your company reacts when you mention “we should try AI”? – B) Open with caution – need more info

How often do teams share tips and help each other with new tools? – D) Always

What best describes your leadership team’s current AI maturity? – C) Formalized policies and oversight in place

What best describes your organization’s overall AI journey? – B) Established practices but inconsistent scale

At what stage of change adoption is your organization regarding AI? – E) AI is embedded in our culture

children.text: Please note:
The detailed graphical report will be more comprehensive and customised. This is a general overview.
{{AI Overview}}
(Your detailed graphic report may take a few minutes to arrive.)
Please note:
These answers and the AI Leadership Roadmap was generated automatically and has not been reviewed. We cannot guarantee its accuracy and assume no liability for its content.
